decisionBodies: DFO Yukon and Transboundary Rivers (egg collection/transfers)

projectScope.activities:
Access

Use of helicopter

A-Star for transportation of personnel and gear (via Dawson City)
EC-120 for transportation for fry release (from Whitehorse via Dawson City) 
Crew may be transferred to active working sites by helicopter 

Total number of flights dependent on the weather conditions

An existing heli-pad will be used at the camp location

Open areas and gravel bars will be used for landing near active working sites, vegetation clearing is avoided

Flight paths will avoid Bear Cave Mountain grizzly bear denning areas
Any wildlife observed during flight operations will be avoided to the fullest extent possible


Safety

A comprehensive safety plan will be in place for:

Weather conditions
Wildlife interactions
Swift water and flooding conditions
Work with and around helicopters

Field activities suspended if safety becomes a concern
Experienced personnel

Camp Use and Crew

The Project will make use of an existing Fisheries and Oceans Canada (DFO) field camp
Up to 3 people
Bear safety measures are in place 

Bear fencing 
Bear-proof food and waste storage

Waste will be incinerated on site or removed
Use of a pit latrine (outhouse) for human waste


Fall Field Work - Between September 25 and October 25

Site Selection and Preparation

Selection of up to 12 gravel-rich sites
Fine sediment removal to mimic natural redds
Construction of mounded gravel features

Use of shovels, rakes and other hand tools

Conducting stream flow and sediment assessments 
Baseline data collection (e.g. point velocity, Wolman pebble count, temperature logging, etc.)

Chum Capture and Fertilization

Up to 20 females and 20 males 

Captured using dipnets or targeted angling

Use of fish holding tubes/bags

On-site fertilization

In small batches to minimize fish handling time
Up to 12 hours (in 2 to 3-hour batches)
River water used to harden the fertilized eggs (at least 15 minutes)

Up to 0.25 m3/day
 Buckets and tubs will be used to collect water
  Water will be deposited back into the river

Up to 50 000 eggs will be fertilized 

25 000 eggs will be allocated along the 12 selected sites, the remaining 25 000 eggs will be transferred to Whitehorse




Egg Allocation and Incubation

Instream incubation at selected 12 sites using three methods:

Whitlock-Vibert Boxes 

Small perforated plastic box (up to 300 eggs per box)
Buried within artificial redds

Closed Mesh Bags 

Soft mesh pouches (30 × 15 × 10 cm; up to 300 eggs per bag)
Filled with gravel and eggs
For monitoring of eggs to fry survival 

Egg Insertion Pipes

Eggs (up to 300) will be deposited directly into gravel mounds with PVC pipes

Maximum seven total incubators per site
All instream incubators will be marked with GPS and flagging tape

Off-site incubation

Gamete are transported to EDI’s Whitehorse facility with a cooler

Incubated in a Portable Egg Eyeing Station
Controlled conditions until unfed fry stage is reached
Disease screening and handling protocols will follow DFO guidance. 




Spring Field Work - Between March 25 to April 30


Fry flown back to site for direct release
Instream incubation egg survival survey

Manual excavation of bed material and artificial redds to retrieve incubation equipment
projectScope.summary:
The Project is a research and restoration-focused initiative to evaluate the feasibility of using instream egg incubation and portable egg eyeing stations to support the restoration of chum salmon spawning in the Fishing Branch River and its tributaries in the Fishing Branch River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 130 km south of Old Crow and 100 km east of Eagle Plains. The Project will be accessed by helicopter. 
The Project is within Vuntut Gwitchin First Nation Territory and overlaps with Settlement Land parcels VGNF S-3A1 and R5a. The Project falls within the North Yukon Planning Region Land Management Unit: Ni'iinlii'njik (Fishing Branch) SMA and Subunit: Ni'iinlii'njik Protected Area. The Project also overlaps Ni'iinlii Njik (Fishing Branch) Territorial Park (Ecological Reserve and Wilderness Preserve). The Project activities are proposed to occur over a 10 day period in late September to late October, followed by a four day period in late March 25 to late April 30 the following year.  These activities can occur sometime between 2026 and 2029.

records.title: Chum Salmon Restoration Trial - Fishing Branch River

Fuel and waste storage

projectScope.summary: The Project is the restoration of the S.S. Klondike vessel, construction of an access boardwalk to the Atlin Barge, and removal of contaminated materials from the S.S. Klondike Historical Site. The Project is on the Territories of the Kwanlin Dün First Nation and Ta’an Kwäch’än Council and within the City of Whitehorse. All activities will take place within the footprint of the S.S. Klondike Historical Site. Project activities are proposed to commence in the spring of 2025 and continue for 5 years.

projectScope.summary: The purpose of the Project is to upgrade the existing North 60 Petro Ltd. fuel service station and bulk fuel plant at 1257 Alaska Highway in the Town of Watson Lake. The Project includes replacing the existing bulk fuel plant with a new fuel plant containing four – 120 000 litre (L) tanks. The current fuel service station will be upgraded with new pumps and one – 120 000 L storage tank, for a total storage capacity of 600 000 L. The bulk fuel storage will contain diesel and gasoline. The bulk fuel plant will be constructed within a secondary containment consisting of a lined and bermed facility. The Project is located within Kaska Territory (Liard First Nation and Ross River Dena Council as defined by YESAA). Project activities will begin in Spring or Fall 2026, with construction taking approximately four months to complete.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the establishment and operation of a placer mine on 2 claims, along Germaine Creek in the Klondike River watershed. The Project is located approximately 25 km southeast of Dawson, within Land Management Unit 12: Tr’ondëk Täk’it (Klondike Valley) of the Final Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan and within Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in Territory. The Project will be accessed using an existing off-claim road which connects to the Klondike Highway. Project activities will occur between April 1 to October 31, annually, for up to 10 years.

The Project is the construction of a multi-use trail in the Dawson City Midnight Dome area. It is located in Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in Territory, approximately 1.5 km from Dawson City. The trail will be a two-way hiking and one-directional climbing bike trail between the existing Acklen Ditch trail and the Midnight Dome summit trail. The trail will be up to 2.4 km long and 1.5 m wide. Final dimensions and routing will be determined during the construction phase. The Project will be accessed via existing roads and trails in the Midnight Dome area and use two existing areas on public access roads for staging project activities. Project activities are anticipated to start on June 1, 2025, and be completed by September 30, 2025.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of a placer mining operation on 16 claims and 2 bench claims along Barlow Creek in the Stewart River Watershed. Diamond drilling up to 150 drill holes totaling 60 000 m in length

Up to 25 drill holes per claim, maximum 50 drill holes per year
When practical, low-impact track-mounted drills and support equipment will be used to minimize ground disturbance
All drill holes marked with a location post or flagging and stemmed or plugged to minimize a groundwater infiltration path
No drill holes or sumps within the 30 m ordinary high-water mark of a waterbody/water course
Only biologically inert and biodegradable drilling muds and additives will be used during the drilling process
Drilling fluids will be directed to a natural sump or constructed sump to allow the drill fluid to naturally exfiltrate
Drilling fluids will be re-circulated when possible and all drill fluids will be contained within the sump

Core storage

All drill cores will be secured and stored in accordance with Section 27 of the Yukon Quartz Mining Land Use Regulation, to prevent physical disturbance or collapse
Core boxes covered with plywood or a similar durable material


Trenching

Up to 10 trenches; total volume of 1 800 m3

Up to 30 m x 3 m x 2 m
Cut with mechanized equipment
Trenches sloped at one end to prevent wildlife entrapment
Trenches reclaimed upon completion of geological mapping and sampling 


Vegetation Clearing for Exploration

Drilling (60 drill sites)

121 m2 of vegetation clearing per drill site 

7 260 m2 total 

64 m2 of each drill site will require removal of vegetative mat

 3 840 m2 total

If vegetative mat is present, it will be removed and stored separately, then replaced after seasonal operations
Most proposed/possible drill sites located well above the tree line and do not require any preparation

New corridors for water lines: up to 4000 m2

No disturbance of the vegetative mat
Corridors will only be brushed out and cutting trees greater than 10 cm in diameter will be avoided

Lichen-rich areas avoided 

If lichen is disturbed, it will be retained and replaced as soon as exploration activities stop

Vegetative mat material removed from proposed trails or drill sites will be properly stockpiled, staked, and marked for future reclamation
If the vegetative mat must be removed, it will be stored separately from overburden or bedrock to protect seed and rootstock for re-establishment after exploration
Clearing native vegetation and topsoil will be minimized to reduce impacts on the surrounding ecosystem
Residual vegetation will be left nearby where it is possible to serve as seed sources and propagule banks
Progressive reclamation of clearings when no longer required

Water Use

Up to 203.5 m3/day sourced from nearby watercourses

Water for camp use: up to 3.5 m3/day
Water for drilling: up to 15 gallons per minute or 200 m3 per day 

Sourced from surface run-off and nearby creek headwater run-off and managed using natural or constructed sumps 
Sumps: 2 m long x 1 m wide and 1 m deep
Sumps constructed with sloped sides or ramps to provide wildlife egress or secured with fencing or netting to prevent wildlife access
All sumps marked during use and then backfilled and reclaimed at the end of each season


Appropriate fish screens and/or guards used on intake pumps
All pumps housed in a secondary containment structure
Apply best practices described in Best Management Practices for Works Affecting Water in Yukon to ensure protection of water quality
Water flow downstream of any water intake or extraction points will be monitored to ensure there is sufficient flow to avoid alteration, disruption, or destruction of fish habitat
Monitoring of water flow downstream of any water intake or extraction points 

Existing Camp: Up to 30 people

One existing camp located on claim YC02291; up to 3 000 person days per year
Existing 3 000 m2 camp; no clearing required for camp establishment
All grey water collected in a sump downwind and away from camp
Camp consists of wall tents, kitchen, dining area, first aid area, privies, generator shacks
Potable water sourced from surface water on YB42184

Drinking water will comply with the Canadian Drinking Water Quality Guidelines

Water treated by filtration


Consultation with the Regional Biologist and the Conservation Officer planned to help determine the level of bear activity in the area and the need for an electrified fence
Seasonal camp closure

All camp structures kept locked and/or secured
Signage posted with company contact information at the camp entrance
Wooden tent platforms left in place with all tents and tarps removed from site
All food, garbage and other wildlife attractants removed and disposed of at appropriate facilities in town


Waste Management 

All waste temporarily stored at designated locations secured with bear fencing at the camp or work site prior to off-site disposal

Camp and kitchen waste double bagged and stored in a secure bear proof container at least 30m downwind from camp or incinerated

Up to 30 kg of burnable waste incinerated at minimum every two days
Ash disposed of in an ash pit covered with gravel or local colluvium

Non-incinerable waste transported to appropriate facilities in Mayo or Dawson and disposed of 1-2 times per week
Waste petroleum products (oil, lubricants, and contaminated fuel) temporarily stored in suitable containers at a secured location until the waste can be transported to a designated waste petroleum recycling facility 

All waste petroleum products removed from the site on an annual basis to the nearest recycling facility



Fuel Storage: Up to 16 000 L

Up to 10 000 L of diesel, 4 000 L of Jet B fuel, 2 000 L of gasoline, 1 500 lb of propane stored off-site and on-site
Off-site Storage

Up to 8 000 L of diesel in Envirotank

Envirotank supplied by a Dawson-based fuel company and transported empty to a flat, gravel-filled laydown area on claim YE99505 (not held by the Proponent)
A registration for the Envirotank will be obtained from the Dawson Mining Land Use office prior to positioning the tank
Diesel delivered by bulk fuel supplier to the Envirotank at the laydown area
Envirotank delivered and removed annually from the Big Creek laydown area by the fuel supply contractor

Diesel transferred to drums and trucked or slung by helicopter to a fuel berm 300 m west and below the camp (on-site storage)

On-site Storage

Main fuel storage area (300 m west of the camp): Up to 6 400 L of fuel in sealed drums within lined berm area

Jet B fuel: up to 3 400 L (17 drums)
Diesel: up to 1 400 L (seven drums)
Gasoline: up to 1 600 L (eight drums)

Drill sites: Up to 600 L (three drums) of diesel stored in a lined berm area
In camp/helicopter pad: Up to 3 100 L

Jet B fuel: up to 600 L (three drums)
Diesel: up to 600 L (three drums)
Gasoline: up to 400 L (drums or Tidy Tanks)
Propane: up 1 500 lb 


Fuel delivered to site by certified drivers 
Fuel stored > 50 m away from waterbodies
Helicopter refueled at the camp heli-pad
Spill Contingency Plan in place and appropriate spill kits available at all locations where storage, re-fueling and/or fuel transfer will occur
Fuel use monitored to leave as little fuel as possible on site at season end 

Any remaining fuel Diesel or Jet B left in sealed drums in the lined berm area

All vehicles and equipment will be routinely inspected for leaks and kept in clean and good working condition
Re-fueling will occur near the fuel storage area and servicing of trucks and equipment will not occur near any watercourse
Any contaminated soil from spills will be excavated (shovels) and deposited in a KBL bag for relocation at the end of the season to an approved facility to accept the contaminated soil

Use of Heavy equipment
Reclamation

All disturbances reclaimed when no longer required
Some drill pads will not be reclaimed

Select pads will remain in place to support efficient and environmentally responsible exploration planning over multiple field seasons, for example drilling from the same area


Other

Implementation of the following plans and Polices:

Wildlife Management Plan

Project personnel must adhere to maximum posted speed limits of 35 kmph on all Project roads and travel to road and weather conditions
The operation of all vehicles shall be confined to roads and trails
Clearing of new areas will be avoided during the bird nesting window
If clearing of a new area from the beginning of May to mid-August is unavoidable, one week prior to clearing and creating any new disturbance, notify the Project supervisor. Bush clearing can occur once clearance has been received from qualified environmental personnel
A qualified biologist will assess the presence and/or occupancy of bird nests for proposed disturbances prior to the start of work to enable avoidance of the area. Any other wildlife features observed during the bird nest survey will also be identified and recorded
An avoidance-based approach for migratory bird nests will be implemented, including cessation of disruptive activities, species-based buffer zones with no planned activity until young have left the vicinity of the nest

Ground Disturbance Management Plan

All surface disturbances will comply with the Operating Conditions contained in Schedule 1 of the Quartz Mining Land Use Regulation
Exploration work where possible will be planned to utilize existing access or open areas wherever possible to minimize new disturbances
Access trails requiring construction will be built carefully to avoid grubbing and blading
Use sumps, silt fencing, ditching, re-sloping/re-contouring, and revegetation to control erosion
Arrange windrows of slash perpendicular to slopes to trap sediment
Clean hay bales used with silt fencing to create more resilient sediment traps
Construction of benches on slopes to control erosion
Construction of drainage ditches along the bottom of highly erosive piles or slopes to carry silt-laden run-off to sediment traps (sumps)
Maintain vegetated buffer strips or setbacks between natural stream boundaries and project works
Riparian buffers will be left intact, with no works occurring within 30 m of a water body

Caribou Management Plan

The Project will interact with caribou during the fall rut. However, the strict operating season ending September 15 will ensure limited to no interactions occur
Caribou will always have the right-of-way. Vehicle operators may resume driving once caribou have crossed the road of their own accord
Project personnel are prohibited from encouraging caribou to cross the roads or from scaring them away from the road (e.g., honk the horn) except under rare circumstances where failure to do so could result in harm to caribou or project personnel
If caribou are encountered in an active work area (e.g., drill site, land clearing), all operational activities shall be minimized or ceased according to operational requirements, such as the health and safety of project personnel. Work may resume to full activity once the caribou have left the work area on their own accord
Project personnel cannot approach or encourage caribou to leave the vicinity of an active worksite except under rare circumstances where failure to do so could result in harm to caribou or project personnel. Any known attempts to harass caribou will be subject to corrective actions
When caribou are observed on an active claim (e.g., where high-intensity activities are occurring) during caribou-sensitive frames (fall rut), activities will be suspended until the caribou leave the area on their own
Will comply with Flying in Caribou Country guidelines (e.g. appropriate altitudes for specific timing windows and for when caribou are observed, adjustment of flight routes when caribou are in area, helicopters will not be used to view caribou, shortened helicopter use window to prevent disturbance to caribou in project area
The Project will provide all seasonal flight path operations annually to Yukon Government
Project infrastructure that is not directly related to exploration activities will be located outside preferred caribou habitats wherever possible, as well as outside of a 1 000 m buffer from mineral licks
Potential road and trail routes will be reconnoitered to select paths that minimize disturbance to high-value or sensitive caribou habitats (e.g., high-use trails, dense lichen mats)
The Proponent has provided a decision tree for vehicle response associated with caribou road crossings (caribou within 100 m of road, caribou beyond 100 m of road)

Violence and Harassment Policies

The Florin Gold Project will provide a workplace for all employees, contractors, clients, and visitors that is free from violence by establishing preventative measures, holding perpetrators of the acts accountable and by aiding and support to those who are impacted by workplace violence
Employer will provide treatment provision to prevent / reduce the potential for a worker to suffer an acute reaction (i.e. mental stress) to a traumatic event would be required, in confidence, by the employer. Employer is required to advise any worker who is subject to violence or who witnesses a violent incident of the availability of immediate debriefing
Employees shall place safety as their highest concern and shall report all acts of violence and threats of violence
All reports of violence will be handled in a confidential manner, with information released only on a need-to-know basis
Implementation of hostile actions standard operating procedures that requires employees to report threats immediately, follow supervisor guidance, engage emergency services as needed, complete incident reports, and participate in debriefings and training to ensure personal safety

Heritage Resources

If heritage finds are discovered, work will cease, the area marked with a minimum 30 m buffer, and the Project Supervisor will be contacted
If any heritage resources are discovered the Heritage Department of the Na-Cho Nyak Dun First Nation and the Yukon Government’s Heritage Branch will be notified
Adherence to applicable legislation to minimize effects to heritage resources (Historic Resources Act (s. 64), Archeological Sites Regulation (s.4), FNNND FA CH 13 – Heritage, Quartz Mining Act, Quartz Mining Land Use Regulations (Section E)
Adherence to the following documents the Mineral Exploration Best Management Practices for Heritage Resources, Best Management Practices for Burial Sites and Human Remains in Yukon
Care will be taken to avoid disturbance of artifacts and sites of heritage and historical significance
Future areas that have not been examined by the HROA and HRIA will proactively manage and mitigate any disturbance to historical and/or heritage sites

Wetlands

Conducting work within ecologically sensitive areas such as wetlands will be avoided

Other Users

Signage will be posted on the road at the project boundary to inform potential hunters that there is a camp and active exploration in the area
At least thirty (30) days prior to each field season and communicate the timing of exploration activities to the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyak Dun and other land and resource users that may access the project area. Florin will work with these individuals and governments to identify and resolve potential land use conflicts and any potential effects on other users
Site personnel will also adhere to a no hunting policy that will eliminate any competition with local hunters
Care will be taken to not disturb or scatter wildlife in the area during exploration activities and air support will avoid low passes or hovering over game animals and hunting parties

Biodiversity

To prevent the spread of invasive species and potential soils, all equipment entering the site must be clean of soils and seeds. The equipment will be inspected prior to entering the project area and then allowed to enter
projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of a ground-based and heli-supported quartz exploration program on 500 claims known as the Florin Gold Project, in central Yukon. It is located approximately 55 km northwest of Mayo, within the Territory of the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyak Dun. The Project is accessed via an existing 95 km network of existing roads (Clear Creek – Big Creek – Hobo Creek roads) connecting to the Klondike Highway at km 610. Project activities will take place from May 15 to September 15, annually, for a period of 5 years. Late winter water quality monitoring will also occur intermittently during this time.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of a placer mine on 8 claims, along Sulphur Creek and Right Limit No-Name Pup in the Indian River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 52 km southeast of Dawson City, within Land Management Unit 11: Goldfields of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan. The Project is located in the Territories of the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in and First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun. Access to the project location will occur via the Goldfields road network. Project activities will occur between April 15 to October 15, annually, for 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of a placer mine on 24 claims along Boutellier Creek, a tributary of Christmas Creek within the White River Watershed. The project is located approximately 10 km southeast of Silver City and 50 km northwest of Haines Junction within Champagne and Aishihik First Nations Territory. Project activities will occur between April 1 and October 31 annually for a period of 10 years.

projectScope.summary:
The Project is the continuation of a placer mining operation on 239 claims along Montana Creek and tributaries in the Indian River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 45 km southeast of Dawson City, within Land Management Unit 11: Goldfields of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan and within the Territories of the Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in and the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyak Dun. The Project will be accessed via an existing network of roads, including the Whitehorse Overland Trail. Project activities will occur between April 1 and October 31, annually, for up to 10 years.
*Scope changes are in bold and are from IR 2 Response (YOR# 2025-0087-0042). The Map Package has been updated accordingly.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the demolition of the existing Tagé Cho Hudän Interpretive Centre and the construction of a new Little Salmon/Carmacks First Nation (LSCFN) Cultural Centre and related infrastructure. The Project will take place on Settlement Land parcels LSC C-4B and LSC C-47FS, Lot 133 and the highway right-of-way, within the municipality of Carmacks and the Territory of LSCFN. Construction activities are expected to occur over three years with reclamation in the summer following construction.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of placer mining on 22 claims along Revenue Creek and Whirlwind Pup, in the Big Creek Placer Watershed. The Project is located approximately 63 km southwest of Pelly Crossing and 57 km northwest of Carmacks and will be accessed via an existing road network (Freegold Road and Casino Trail). The Project is located within the Territories of the Little Salmon/Carmacks First Nation and Selkirk First Nation. Project activities are scheduled to occur from March 1 to November 15, annually, for 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continued operation and maintenance of the existing Terus Construction Ltd. Quarry and asphalt plant. The Project was previously assessed in 2014, YOR: 2014-0176, with the current air emission permit expiring on December 31, 2024. The project site is located within the municipal boundaries of Whitehorse. Access to the Project is from Ear Lake Road off Robert Service Way. The Project is located within the Territories of Kwanlin Dün First Nation and Ta’an Kwäch’än Council. The proposed Project involves the extraction, crushing, hauling of aggregate materials, asphalt production and associated activities. Mining of aggregate reserve over the next 10 years will be at a rate of 10 000 m3 per year. The asphalt plant is expected to produce between 20 000 to 40 000 tonnes of material per year. On-site crushing will take place up to 2 times per year for a period of 1-3 weeks. Proposed activities will occur seasonally between May to the beginning of October and will continue for 10 years (2025-2035)

projectScope.summary: The Project is a class 3 quartz exploration program near Clear Creek in the Little South Klondike River drainage. The Project consists of a total of 1 052 contiguous claims over a distance of 44 km that will be accessed via the Clear Creek Road (for approximately 23 km) and by aircraft. The Project is approximately 66 km northwest of Mayo and 119 km southeast of Dawson, within Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in and First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun Territory. Part of the project area falls within Land Management Unit 9: Clear Creek of the Recommended Dawson Land Use Plan. Project activities are proposed to occur from June 1 to September 15 annually for 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of a placer mine on 228 grants along Sprague Creek and its tributaries in the McQuesten River watershed. The Project is located approximately 50 km northwest of Mayo, within the Territory of the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun. Access to the project will occur by Clear Creek Road off the Klondike Highway. Project activities will occur between March 1 and October 31 annually for up to 10 years.

Project activities are scheduled to occur from May 1 to October 30, annually, for 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The Project is an amendment to an existing placer mine on 31 claims (land use permit AP23031) previously assessed by the Designated Office in 2023 (YESAB Project 2023-0114). The Project is located along Browns Creek and five unnamed tributaries, in the Fortymile River watershed. The proposed amendment is the addition of one bench claim (P 532338) to relocate the camp and fuel storage facility. The Project is located approximately 80 km north-west of Dawson City in Trʼondëk Hwëchʼin Territory. The Project falls within the Land Management Unit 21: Wëdzey Tay (Fortymile Caribou Corridor) of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan. The Project will be accessed via an existing access road. Project activities will take place from March 15 to October 15, annually, until licence expiry date (2034).

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of a placer mine on 14 claims along Upper Bonanza Creek and Rainbow Gulch in the Klondike River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 35 km southeast of Dawson City, within Land Management Unit 11: Goldfields of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan, and within Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in Territory. Access to the project location will occur via the Goldfields road network, as well as by existing and new roads and trails. Project activities will occur between April 1 to October 15, annually, for 10 years.

records.title: Placer Mine - Upper Bonanza Creek and Rainbow Gulch

projectScope.activities:
Timing and Phasing of Activities

Structure removal activities will be scheduled outside of the campground visitor season
Work will be schedules to occur on hard frozen ground whenever possible
Phase one: will occur during late winter/spring 2026. Activities include:

Site preparation and structure analysis

Expected to require approximately three days
Removing internal/external items (not structures)

Testing for hazardous materials

Phase two: will occur during late winter/spring 2026, exact dates dependent on weather conditions when ground is frozen. Activities include:

Structure removal and disposal based on hazardous testing results

Expected to require up to seven days
Non-hazardous materials: deconstructed and removed via mechanical means across frozen snow
Hazardous materials: professional abatement will be used
Outhouses at the site will be capped and decommissioned


Phase three: will occur during spring/summer 2026. Activities include:

Final cleanup and site remediation (if required)
Ecological monitoring for invasive species

Conducted in snow-free conditions (monitoring duration of summer season)
100 m buffer surrounding the project footprints to capture potential indirect impacts to the adjacent aquatic environments



Structure Removal and Clearing

Eight structures have been designated into four groups for removal

Group One: 

One structure (200 ft2) and outhouse
Tree clearing: up to 10 m3

Group Two: 

Two structures (360 ft2 and 200 ft2)
Tree clearing: up to 10 m3

Group Three: 

One main structure (675 ft2) and one shed (80 ft2)
Tree clearing: up to 5 m3
Due to the proximity to water, additional surveillance monitoring will occur during removal and will only occur during dry or frozen conditions
Erosion control measures shall be implemented prior to start of work in areas 30 m of watercourse
Wastes from demolition, including, rock, silt, cement, grout, asphalt, petroleum product, lumber, vegetation, domestic waste, pesticide, herbicide or any deleterious substance will be placed or allowed to disperse into waterbody
If waste does fall into a waterbody, it will be immediately retrieved, provided worker safety is not compromised, and if removal can be done without excessive disturbance of bottom sediment

Group Four: 

One main structure (288ft2) and one shed (187 ft2)
Tree clearing: up to 10 m3


Debris Removal and Material Transportation

Sorting and piling materials for salvage or disposal
No fill is expected to be used
Ground disturbance will be minimal
Some tree clearing may be required to allow for heavy equipment mobility


Use of Heavy Equipment

Equipment from outside the national park will be washed and clean before arrival and free of contaminants
Equipment will be free of leaks and in good working order
Equipment staging will occur on previously disturbed, compacted areas (i.e. parking lot)
Stored a minimum of 30 m from the high-water mark

Fuel and Waste Management

Fuel Type and quantity

Diesel: up to 400 L
If necessary, fuel storage will be a tidy tank mounted on a truck staged in day-use parking lot a minimum of 30 m away from high-water mark

Refueling to occur a minimum of 30 m away from watercourse, if required

If refueling on-site, location will be flat and compacted ground (pre-existing road/parking lot). Secondary containment (e.g., a duck pond) will be required for any ground-based refueling activities
Spill plan in place and spill kit on-site capable of handling 110 percent of the total fuel
Spill response procedures implemented in the event of a spill and notify designated Parks Canada staff

Waste to be contained and transported to an approved facility (Haines Junction or Whitehorse)
Proper waste management practices will be followed

Secure all food, waste, and debris in wildlife-proof containers, a secure building, or vehicles


Access and Access Control

Equipment laydown and staging on site will be the designated day-use parking lot
Light brushing may be required for heavy equipment access
No ground disturbance or groundwork will be associated with heavy equipment access points
Public access around the project site will be restricted during operations

Protection of Wildlife

Bats

No removal of potential roosting structures will occur outside their active period (April to October)
Visual and audio sweep of the buildings will be conducted by designated park personnel prior to work commencing
Conduct activities during daylight hours and avoid critical foraging times (i.e., dusk and dawn) unless otherwise approved by designated Parks Canada staff 
If dead or injured bats are found, a Parks Canada Environmental Surveillance Officer (or designate) will be notified

Avian Wildlife

Removal of trees and potential nesting structures will occur outside the general nesting period for birds (May 2 to August 20)

If wildlife is observed at or near the work site, the animal(s) will be allowed to leave the work area
Any problems including aggressive encounters with wildlife will be reported immediately to the park Wildlife Conflicts Specialist or designated Parks Canada staff

Heritage Resources

Parks Canada’s Cultural Resource Management (CRM) guidelines will be followed
If a heritage resource is discovered, all work will cease in the immediate area, and appropriate specialists and CAFN will be consulted to assess and document the finding
projectScope.summary: The Project is the removal of eight structures along the periphery of the day-use area of Kathleen Lake in Kluane National Park and Reserve (KNPR); including site access, structure deconstruction, debris removal and transporting materials to an approved facility. The Project is located along the Haines Highway, approximately 30 km south of the Village of Haines Junction (Dakwakada), within the Territory of Champagne and Aishihik First Nations (CAFN). The Project will take place between winter/spring 2026 to the end of summer 2026. Should hazardous materials be discovered during testing and demolition, the Project timeline may be delayed up to one year. Accordingly, project activities may occur over a two year period to allow for scheduling of professional abatement of hazardous materials.

projectScope.summary: The Project is an amendment to an existing Class 4 placer mine (PM20-072), previously assessed by the Dawson Designated Office in 2021 (YESAB Project 2021-0034). The proposed amendment includes the addition of eight claims and one lease, for a total of 22 claims and one lease, and includes the addition of new activities and increases to previously assessed activities, which are captured below. The Project is located along Adams Creek, within the Klondike River Watershed.  Accessed via Bonanza Creek Road, the Project is located approximately 10 km south from Dawson City, within the Dawson Land Management Unit 11- Goldfields. The project location is within Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in Territory, with activities on the additional claims proposed to occur on Settlement Land Parcel R-20 A. Project activities are proposed to occur from May 1 to October 30, annually, for six years.

The project is located approximately 14 km southeast of Keno City and within the Territory of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun First Nation. projectScope.summary: The Project is a quartz exploration program in the Freegold Mountain area. The property consists of 198 claims and will be accessed via the Freegold Road from Carmacks. The Project is located within the Territories of the Little Salmon/Carmacks First Nation (LSCFN) and Selkirk First Nation (SFN), approximately 57 km northwest of Carmacks. The Project is adjacent to LSCFN Settlement Land parcel LSC R-8A and overlaps with LSC R-9A. No activities will be taking place on Settlement Land. Project activities are proposed to occur from April 1 to November 30, annually, for five years.

projectScope.summary: The Project is a quartz mining, processing and production program on 1 claim on Mount Follé. Accessed from Annie Lake Road, the Project lies within in the Hamlet of Mount Lorne, approximately 23 km from Carcross and 45 km from Whitehorse. The Project is located in the Territories of Carcross/Tagish First Nation (C/TFN) and Kwanlin Dün First Nation and overlaps Settlement Land parcel CTFN R-4A. Activities are scheduled to occur annually from May 1 to November 30, for up to 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the establishment and operation of a placer mine on 146 claims, along Wounded Moose[RM1.1] Creek and its tributaries in the Indian River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 60 km South of Dawson City within Land Management Unit 17: Nän Dhòhdäl (Upper Indian River Wetlands) of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan and within the Territories of the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in and First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun. Access to the Project will occur by Indian River Road. Project activities will occur between March 1 and November 30, annually, for up to 10 years. Reclamation work will be carried out progressively over the ten-year life of the Project.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of a placer mine on 2 claims along 24 Pup Right Fork Hunker Creek in the Klondike River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 30 km southeast of Dawson within Land Management Unit 11: Goldfields of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan and within the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in Territory. projectScope.summary: The Project is a continuation of a placer mine on 59 claims and 1 lease along Quartz Creek and Calder Creek in the Indian River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 35 km south of Dawson City in the Territories of the Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in and the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun. The Project falls within Land Management Unit 11: Goldfields – Integrated Stewardship Area 4, of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan. The project location will be accessed via the existing Quartz Creek Road. Project activities are proposed from April 1 to November 1, annually, for 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The proposed project involves relocating a section of overhead powerline into a highway right-of-way to improve service reliability. The Project will take place within the municipality of Watson Lake, between approximately km 5 and 7 of the Robert Campbell Highway. The Project is located in the Yukon portion of Kaska territory, within the Territories of the Liard First Nation and Ross River Dena Council. Following installation of the new powerline, the existing powerline will be decommissioned. Activities will take up to 10 weeks to complete over one season between July and October, with work conducted between 7:30 a.m. and 6:30 p.m.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of a quartz exploration program on 92 claims on Barney Ridge, near Clear Creek, approximately 107 km southeast of Dawson City and 70 km northwest of Mayo. The Project is located within the Territories of Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in and the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyak Dun and falls within Land Management Unit 9: Clear Creek - Integrated Stewardship Area 4 of the Dawson Recommended Land Use Plan. Project activities are proposed from January 1 to December 31, annually, for 10 years.

projectScope.summary:
The Project is the continuation of a placer mine on 131 claims, along Rosebute Creek and tributaries in the Yukon River North Watershed. The Project is located approximately 60 km south of Dawson City, within Land Management Unit 11: Goldfields of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan. The Project is located in Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in Territory. Access to the project location will occur via helicopter from Dawson City and by vehicle via the existing Goldfields road network, including Eureka Creek Road and Rosebute Valley Access Road. Project activities will occur between April 1 to October 15, annually, for 10 years.
The Designated Office notes that the Proponent has proposed both Class 3 and Class 4 activities. The Designated Office will assess all activities proposed within one assessment.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the establishment or re-establishment of the Scroggie Creek winter road to transport heavy equipment and materials to a remote placer mine area located on Scroggie Creek. The winter road will be accessed near Pelly Farm via the Pelly Ranch Road, located approximately 37.5 km from Pelly Crossing off the Klondike Highway. The Project is located within the Territories of Selkirk First Nation (SFN), Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in (TH), and First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun (FNNND). A portion of the Project is adjacent to Settlement Land parcel SFN R-20A, and near Settlement Land parcels TH S-209B1, SFN S-86B1/D, SFN S-48B1/D, and SFN R-1B, but no work will occur on Settlement Land. Project activities will occur over up to six trips between December 1 and April 15 (conditions allowing), annually, for 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the construction of an access road that will connect to the existing Gully Road to agricultural lot 1527. The Project is located approximately 25 km northwest of downtown Whitehorse and is within the Territories of the Kwanlin Dün, Ta'an Kwäch'än Council and Champagne and Aishihik First Nations. The Project is near or adjacent to settlement land parcels TKC R-9A and KDFN S-365B1 and falls within the Hot Spring Road Local Area Planning boundary. Project activities are expected to take one month, with clearing beginning in March 2026, and the construction of the access road occurring once ground conditions are dry.

projectScope.summary:
This project is for the construction and operation of a 1,439 kW Biomass-Fueled District Energy System (DES) in the City of Dawson. The Project is located within the Territory of the Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in (TH) and the municipal boundary of the City of Dawson. The project area is zoned Downtown Core under the City of Dawson Official Community Plan and overlaps with Settlement Land parcels TH C-63FS/D, TH C-20FS/D, TH C-21 FS/D, TH C-35FS/D and TH C-26FS/D, as well as TH-owned land, freehold land, and Dawson City-owned land.
Project activities include decommissioning of an existing building, construction of a biomass boiler plant, installation of underground hydronic pipes to distribute heated propylene glycol, and operation of the DES. The expected lifespan of the DES is 20 years. Construction is anticipated to take up to 15 months, occurring between summer 2025 and March 31, 2027.

The Project is located approximately 4 km east of Keno City within the Territory of the Na-Cho Nyäk Dun. projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of a placer mining operation (water licence PM18-017) on 83 claims and a three-mile lease (~32 claims; for a total of ~115 claims) along Nines Creek and tributaries. Activities will take place from June 15 to October 15, annually, for 10 years.

projectScope.summary:
The Project is two adjacent placer mining operations in the Nordenskiold River Watershed:

Operation 1: 27 claims located on a tributary of Rowlinson Creek (UNLLT of Rowlinson Creek)
Operation 2: 24 claims located on a first-order tributary of Rowlinson Creek (Miller Creek) and a second-order tributary of Rowlinson Creek (UNLLT of Miller Creek)

These two operations by the same Proponent have been combined into a single assessment due to proximity and yearly temporal overlap. The Project is located approximately 20 km west of Carmacks in the Territory of the Little Salmon/Carmacks First Nation. No mining activities will occur within 60 m of adjacent Settlement Land parcel LSC S-86B1/D. Access to both operations is via Mount Nansen Road. Project activities will occur annually between April 1 and November 1 for ten years.

projectScope.summary: The Project is a placer mining operation on 27 contiguous claims along Ledge Creek and its tributaries, 1 Mile Pup and No-name Pup #1. Ledge Creek is a right limit tributary of Mayo Lake within the Mayo River Watershed. The claim block is located approximately 52 km straight-line distance northeast of Mayo and 32 km straight-line distance southeast of Keno, with existing access via the Mayo Lake Road to the edge of Mayo Lake, and an additional 30 km across the water to the mouth of Ledge Creek. The Project is located within the Territory of the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyak Dun and completely overlaps with Settlement Land Parcel NND R-2A. Project activities will occur from May 1 to October 31, annually, for 10 years.

projectScope.summary:
Yukon Energy Corporation (YEC) is proposing to conduct trail brushing and maintenance activities along 131 existing access trails connecting the Robert Campbell Highway to the YEC powerline easement (L178). The trailheads are located between km 417 and km 577.6 between the Village of Carmacks and the Town of Faro. Re-establishing these access trails is being proposed to undertake necessary inspection, refurbishment, repair and upgrade to the aging Carmacks/Faro transmission line. In addition, YEC is proposing to clear up to 0.058 hectares near the Pelly River within the Town of Faro in preparation for future re-routing of a portion of transmission lines L178 and L356.
The Project is located within the Territories of the Little Salmon/Carmacks First Nation (LSCFN), Liard First Nation (LFN) and Ross River Dena Council (RRDC). Nine existing access trails overlap with Settlement Land parcels (LSC R-45B, LSC R-58B, and LSC R-56B), seven of which require land use authorization from LSCFN. Activities are proposed during the fall, winter, and spring seasons over one year from permitting. Trail brushing is expected to take approximately 8 weeks and clearing for the transmission line re-routing is expected to take up to 3 weeks. Maintenance activities are expected to continue for the life of the powerline.

records.title: Existing Powerline Access Trail Restoration - Carmacks / Faro Transmission Line (L178)

projectScope.summary: The Project is the exploration and development of 172 grants on Preacher Creek (including a UNLLT to Preacher Creek) and Iron Creek in the Stewart River Placer Watershed. The Project is located approximately 103 km south of Dawson City within the Territory of the Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in and Selkirk First Nation. Access to the project will occur via barge from Dawson City to the Thistle Creek barge landing (Yukon River), Barker Creek barge landing (Stewart River), and by air from Dawson City to the Thistle Creek and Barker Creek airstrips. Project activities are scheduled to occur from April 1 to November 1 annually for 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the establishment of a Class 3 placer mine on 11 claims along 11 Pup. The majority of the Project is in the Yukon River North Watershed, and a small extent is in the Stewart River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 75 km South of Dawson City, within Land Management Unit 11: Goldfields of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan and within Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in Territory. Access to the Project will occur via the existing Henderson Dome and Maisy May Creek roads. Project activities will occur between April 1 to November 15 annually, for up to 10 years.

Erosion control measures may include, but are not limited to, sediment fences, straw bales, poly cover for stockpiles or exposed roadway banks and erosion control matting


Road Construction

Approximately 7 300 m3 of pit run and 3 400 m2 of granular base course used for the project
Granular material, geotextile stabilization and/or rip rap may be required for slope stabilization
Granular material will be sourced from a local gravel pit

Imported granular material will get metal leaching/acid rock draining (ML/ARD) testing completed before use

Material not used immediately will be stockpiled at the staging area, which is < 30 m from the Yukon River

Dust Suppression

Dust control measures implemented based on visual inspections, community complaints, and during high dust generating activities 

Dust control measures to include water spraying as dust suppressant, speed limits in work area, daily inspections, and wheel wash stations for vehicles


Works Involving Existing Infrastructure

Boardwalk (50 m) removed prior to start of project and re-installed once project is completed
Gazebo Removed prior to start of project and re-installed once project is completed

Traffic Management 

Selected contractor to develop and implement a Traffic Management Plan 

The plan will take into consideration specific project activities, the site, public safety and worker safety

The contractor will work with the Village of Carmacks and Little Salmon/Carmacks First Nation to communicate any temporary road closures 
Construction area on River Drive to be blocked with rope and/or barriers

Signage regarding the closure to be installed at either end of River Drive


Site Restoration

Site Restoration will occur once construction activities are completed 
Areas along the slope of the road graded, scarified and seeded with native seeds to encourage revegetation

Use of 3 600 m3 of topsoil/seed
All revegetation activities will be in accordance with the practices described in the Yukon Revegetation Manual


Fuel Storage and Handling 

Fuel will be supplied by truck mounted tidy tanks
A spill contingency plan will be posted on-site during the Project

Workers will be trained in the execution of the spill contingency plan and fuel handling

Spill kits will be on each piece of heavy equipment and at the staging area
Equipment will be kept in good condition and inspected daily 

Waste Management

Domestic solid waste stored in bear-proof bins until disposed of at local solid waste facility
One or more portable toilets will be at the staging area, waste from the portable toilet will be collected by a wastewater truck and disposed of at approved facility

Other

If possible, vegetation will be disturbed or removed outside of the bird nesting period (May 3 to August 20); If vegetation needs to be disturbed or removed within the bird nesting period a qualified professional biologist will carry out a bird survey. If nests are encountered, they will not be disturbed until juvenile birds have fledged
Areas where vegetation is removed/disturbed will be graded and reseeded to promote revegetation
Project activities will be carried out in accordance with the Government of Yukon guidelines Preferred Practices for works affecting Yukon Waters (2019)
Soil and/or imported gravel/rip rap materials will be stored more than 30 m from the Yukon River 
Rip rap material used for road slope and/or culvert stabilization will be washed and non-metal leaching and non-acid generating
Fuel storage and handling will occur a minimum of 30 m from the Yukon River
Spill kits will be present on each piece of heavy equipment and at the staging area
Areas where vegetation is removed and/or disturbed will be graded and reseeded to promote revegetation to reduce potential of erosion
The contractor will work with the Village of Carmacks and Little Salmon/Carmacks First Nation to communicate the planned road upgrades, temporary road closure, and schedule to the community
Access to the Project location will be restricted with signage, barriers and caution tape 
Active construction areas will be left in a safe condition at the end of each workday (i.e. all equipment stored at staging area, signage posted indicating entrance to the site is restricted, no open excavations, no steep slopes, etc.); and if a worker see a member of the public approaching or within the Project area they will direct them away from the Project area.
projectScope.summary: The Project’s purpose is to upgrade a portion of River Drive, in the Village of Carmacks, to protect it from flooding. Upgrades include increasing the surface elevation of River Drive to approximately 120 mm above the 1:200-year flood event elevation. The Project takes place on Little Salmon/Carmacks First Nation Territory and is adjacent to Settlement Land parcel LSC C-18FS. A former highways and public works property will be used as a staging area for materials and equipment. The Project is scheduled to occur between April 1 and October 31 of a given year, dependent on assessment timelines and development permit.

projectScope.summary: The Project is a Class 3 placer mining operation on two claims along the right fork of Hunker Creek in the Klondike River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 30 km southeast of Dawson City, within Land Management Unit 11: Goldfields of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan and within Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in Territory. Access to the Project will occur by Hunker Road and the right fork of Hunker Road. Project activities will occur from May 15 to September 15, annually, for up to five years.

projectScope.summary: The Project involves the development of a fuel break and service roads, as well as selective timber harvesting to increase fire resistance. The Project is located within Teslin Tlingit Council (TTC) Territory, overlapping with multiple TTC Settlement Land parcels and lying within and to the north and west of the municipality of Teslin. The timber harvest area between the fuel break and the Alaska Highway corresponds with the Phase 2 treatment area of the Teslin Community Wildlife Protection Plan (CWPP). Project activities will take up to 15 years to complete, followed by ongoing maintenance.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of a placer mine on 159 claims and lease IW00870 on Canadian Creek, Britannia Creek and unnamed tributaries at the boundary of the Yukon River South Watershed and the White River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 140 km south of Dawson City and 100 km northwest of Minto, within the Territories of Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in and Selkirk First Nation. The Project will be accessed by barge on the Yukon River from Minto or Dawson City or by air to an existing airstrip. The Project will take place from April 1 to November 15, annually, for 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the repair of Piers 3 and 4 of the Teslin River Bridge along the Alaska Highway at Johnsons Crossing, approximately 50 km northwest of Teslin. Bridge replacement activities will occur within the highway right-of-way and an existing bridgehead reservation (Disposition 105C06-055). The Project is located within the Territory of the Teslin Tlingit Council (TTC), directly adjacent to Settlement Land parcels TTC C-36B and TTC R-11B with access using existing roads under a specified access right through Settlement Land parcel TTC C-36B. Project activities will occur year-round over up to five years between July 2026 and 2035.

projectScope.summary: The Project is a grouped project of two individual placer mines on 225 claims along Sixty Mile River, Unnamed Right Limit Tributary (UNRLT) 5, and 12 Mile Creek in the Sixty Mile River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 48 km west of Dawson City, within Land Management Unit 15 Khel Dëk (Sixty Mile) – Integrated Stewardship Area 3 of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan and within the Territory of the Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in. The Project will be accessed via the existing Top of the World Highway, from Swede Creek Dome to the top end of California Creek, or from km 88 to the top end of the claim block along the Sixty Mile River. The Project will involve the creation of new roads, trails, and fords. Project activities are proposed to occur from March 1 to October 31, annually, for up to 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The Proponent is proposing timber harvest on 2 Operating Units (OU) in the 2025 Bear Creek II Timber Harvest Plan area. The Project is located east of the Alaska Highway, approximately 20 km north of Haines Junction on Champagne & Aishihik First Nations  Territory. OU 1 will be accessed between December and April, via km 1592 of the Alaska Highway; OU 2 has the potential for year-round harvest and will be accessed via an existing gravel pit near km 1603.5 on the Alaska Highway. Project activities will begin in the fall of 2025 or as permits are obtained and will occur over 10 years. Silvicultural activities may occur for an additional 10 years based on regeneration assessments.

Water pump refuelling minimum 5 m from watercourse 
Fuel spill kits and trays readily available with fuel delivery trucks and at fueling and storage locations

Existing Camp

On claim P 39957; up to 10 personnel

Composition- six RV’s and trailers, four well-site trailers, sea cans and up to four pit privies.
Located 30 m from nearest watercourse


Waste Management

Daily kitchen waste burned to ash in a barrel if the amount is less than 5 kg per day
Non-hazardous mine waste temporarily stored in a secure containment area on site, transported every two weeks to an authorized disposal facility and/or municipal waste disposal site in Dawson City and/or Whitehorse
Waste stored in wildlife-proof containment 
Hazardous waste will be stored in approved containers that are clearly labelled and marked, and kept in secure locations that are protected from the elements and elevated off the ground. 
Small amounts of hazardous waste transported every two weeks to facility in Dawson City, other hazardous waste will be transported to Whitehorse 
All wastes stored at locations minimum 30 m from watercourse

Timber Use
projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of a placer mine on 22 claims, along Dominion Creek in the Indian River watershed. The Project is located approximately 36 km southeast of Dawson, within Land Management Unit 11 of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan and within the Territories of Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in and the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyak Dun. Access to the Project will occur by existing roads, including the Dominion Creek Road. Project activities will occur between April 15 and October 31, annually, for 10 years.

projectScope.activities:
Use of Heavy Equipment

Temporary Access and Site Preparation
Clearing within the right-of-way for equipment access
Construction of temporary leveled work areas for safe drill operation 
Grubbing activities to remove vegetative mat for access
The Contractor will avoid placing stockpiles within 10 m from riparian areas or watercourses
Hand tools (e.g., chainsaws) will be used to clear or remove vegetation within 10 m of the natural boundary of any waterbody or watercourse
Following completion, disturbed areas will be recontoured and reclaimed

Geotechnical Drilling

Use of sonic, auger, vibratory, rotary, mud rotary, or diamond drill rigs (truck- or track-mounted)
Up to 500 boreholes annually with up to 7 500 boreholes for the life of the Project
Boreholes up to 30 cm in diameter and 100 m in depth
Boreholes will be 200 km apart with up to 5 boreholes/km; up to 20 boreholes/km for bridge and culvert locations
Up to three drill rigs operating concurrently throughout the project area
Drilling may occur up to the ordinary high-water mark, where required to assess bridge or culvert infrastructure
Boreholes will be located at least 2 m away from underground utilities and a 7 m setback distance from overhead powerlines
Boreholes will be backfilled immediately after completion to prevent entrapment or injury to wildlife

Water use

Up to 100 m3/day sourced from streams, waterbodies and municipal systems

For purposes of:

Up to 60 m3/day for drill rig operation; and
General site water management i.e. dust control


Water pumps will be located at a minimum of 2m from the ordinary high-water mark

Placed within secondary containment capable of holding 100% of the pump's fluid capacity

All water pump intakes will include fish screens

Fuel and Equipment Management

Diesel: up to 5 000 L (one storage tank; up to two fuel trucks and tidy tanks)
Gasoline: up to 1 000 L (1 storage tank and tidy tanks)
Propane: up to 1 000 L 
Oils and lubricants: up to 500 L
Refuelling will occur over containment trays > 30 m of a watercourse
Water pumps refuelled using jerry cans or tidy tanks from the back of pickup trucks and a spill kit will be kept beside all water pumps
Spill kits and fire extinguishers will be located at stationary fuel storage areas and within mobile equipment
Equipment staging and fuel storage, if required, will occur within designated gravel pits or within the highway right-of-way in a manner that prevents release to the surrounding environment

Waste Management

No long-term storage of waste anticipated
All waste will be disposed of at an approved facility either in a community or in Whitehorse
Domestic waste will be stored within vehicles and disposed of daily at the accommodation site or at an approved disposal facility within nearby communities
For remote project locations, domestic waste will be stored in metal, wildlife-proof containers and disposed of at an approved facility at least once a week
Special waste (e.g., waste oil, lubricants) will be stored in airtight or steel locking containers

Traffic Management and Safety

The Contractor will prepare a Traffic Management Plan for review by HPW prior to starting work
All construction zones, speed limits, route markers and warning signs will be clearly posted
A notice of construction will be posted on Yukon 511 to inform the public of potential traffic interruptions

Accommodations

No camps proposed
Contractors will utilize commercial or private accommodations

Progressive Reclamation
Further Mitigations

Environmental Management Plan

Each Contractor is required to submit a project specific Environmental Management Plan (EMP) to HPW for review before the project starts
The EMP will outline environmental protection measures including but not limited to erosion and sediment control; fuel and spill management; contaminated soil testing, storage, removal or treatment procedures; water and wastewater use; water truck loading areas and pumping operations; procedures to test for and take leaking equipment out of service; garbage disposal and litter control; and protection of natural resources and wildlife
Erosion, Sedimentation and Contamination
Clearing will be completed in the winter or fall 
Erosion and sediment control measures (e.g., sediment fences) will be installed

Health and Safety for Workers

The Contractor shall prepare a Project Specific Safety Plan for review by HPW prior to starting the work

Heritage and Archaeological Resources

Prior to ground-disturbing activities, a Heritage Resource Overview Assessments will be reviewed or completed where none exist
Areas identified as known or newly discovered heritage resources will be avoided and flagged with a 30 m buffer, unless otherwise directed by the YG Heritage
projectScope.summary:
The Project is a territory-wide geotechnical drilling program within all Yukon highway rights-of-ways (ROW) listed in Schedule 1 of the Yukon Highways Regulations and associated bridgehead reserves under the jurisdiction of the Government of Yukon, Department of Highways and Public Works (HPW).
Activities will include soil and core sampling to characterize subsurface conditions in support of future activities such as highway and bridge design, repair, maintenance, and reconstruction (not included in this project.) Drilling will occur on existing road embankments or elsewhere within the right-of-way, including adjacent bridges and culverts. Project activities will occur year-round, for up to 15 years.
No work will take place on private land or Settlement Land.
To see the First Nation Territories this project overlaps with, please see the map package located on the YESAB Online Registry (YOR).

projectScope.activities:
Project Scope was updated January 12, 2026 - See bolded area
Access

Access to the project area is via the Alaska Highway and McLean Lake Road

Land Treatment Facility (LTF) Operation

General operations

Continued operations within the 15 ha land treatment facility leased area (9 ha currently undeveloped and 6 ha developed)
Accommodation of up to 70 000 m3 of contaminated materials
Berms and diversion ditches divert surface water around the LTF, while on-site meltwater and rainwater evaporate or infiltrate into the biopiles

Transport and acceptance of materials to the LTF

Transport of contaminated materials to LTF by private companies

Only materials authorized under a Yukon Government–issued relocation permit or covered under an environmental protection order in the case of an emergency cleanup, will be accepted

Transport of contaminated materials to LTF by Arctic Backhoe

Use of suitable cover to prevent release of contaminated soil during transport
Use of appropriate tanker truck to prevent release of contaminated liquid during transport
Review of laboratory analytical results to verify treatability prior to transport
Notification of Yukon Government as part of the relocation permit application process
Maintenance of records in accordance with regulatory requirements
Adherence to all terms of the relocation permit and/or environmental protection order


Contaminated soil deposition and treatment

Soil treatment area is currently designed to accommodate a capacity of approximately 30 000 m3 of soil
Contaminated soil dumped into windrows and then tilled into biopiles up to 4 m high
A new windrow or separate soil stockpile is created for each new shipment of contaminated soil to prevent mixing of soils from different sites
Soil from smaller stockpiles mixed based on analytical results 

Mixed stockpiles will not exceed 500 m³ per stockpile

Emergency spill materials from the Whitehorse area may be temporarily stored in the soil treatment area, within a segregated zone, concrete cell, or above-ground storage tanks while contaminant testing is completed

Materials meeting Yukon Contaminated Sites Regulation Park land use standards will be removed without treatment

Spills or leaks with soil hydrocarbon concentrations greater than 30 000 ppm are treated as highly contaminated under the current operating permit

Highly contaminated soil placed in a concrete holding cell for pre-treatment
Holding cells are sloped to drain liquid through a valved screening mechanism into underground liquid containment tanks
Sloped concrete pads have a maximum capacity of approximately 125 m³ at 85% full
Pre-treatment methods include frequent tilling, addition of nitrogen-based fertilizer and use of modified biopile system
Once analytical results show the soil is no longer highly contaminated, it is relocated to the soil treatment area

Natural remediation is used to treat hydrocarbon-contaminated soils, typically completing within two to five years

Biopiles regularly tilled (typically bimonthly) to promote biodegradation

Soils that have been treated, sampled, and approved for reuse will be stockpiled within the lease area (LTF and/or quarry) 

Sump waste deposition and treatment

Sump waste includes waste from garage sumps, wash bays, and city storm sewers and not intended to contain human waste
Sludge from sump waste is placed into the lagoons
Once the holding cell or liquid containment tank reaches capacity, its contents are batch-tested before being transferred to biopiles or shipped to an approved facility, depending on metal concentrations

Contaminated snow deposition and treatment 

Contaminated snow or ice is placed in a holding cell or in one of two lagoons (each <600 m2)
During spring, contaminated snowmelt is collected in liquid containment tanks connected to the holding cell or directly into a lagoon
Contaminated meltwater is sprayed onto biopiles as required, and contamination is remediated along with the soil in the biopile

Contaminated liquid deposition and treatment

Liquids are pumped into one of nine above-ground storage tanks or into one of two lagoons for storage

Temporary storage tanks range in capacity from 19 000 L (5 000 gal) to 78 000 L (20 000 gal), with a total capacity of 341 000 L (90 000 gal)

Each shipment of contaminated liquid is stored separately
If analytical results show liquids do not exceed CSR Protection of Aquatic Life Standards, they may be sprayed onto biopiles or pumped into a liquid holding cell

If contaminated liquids are sprayed onto biopiles, they are remediated with contaminated soil
If liquids are found to be unacceptable for treatment (e.g., highly contaminated with metals), they are shipped to a licensed facility outside the Yukon for treatment


Water use

Up to 100 m3 of water per year from municipal sources, transported and stored in steam trucks, and used to steam clean on-site tanks and spray biopiles

On-site tanks for recycling/reuse

Storage or reuse of up to 30 tanks from the remediation of contaminated sites (e.g., household underground storage tanks)
Cleaned prior to arrival or cleaned at the LTF 
Cleaned tanks may be transported to spill sites and used to collect contaminated liquids for treatment at the LTF
Stored up to three years or recycled at a facility outside of Yukon

Monitoring and testing

Sampling and requesting laboratory analyses of soil, snow, liquid, and sludge before, during, and after the remediation process
Inspecting, maintaining, and testing the compacted glacial till silt liner

The liner is tested after each biopile is remediated and removed from the treatment cell to confirm it is not contaminated.
Any contaminated liner material within the facility is excavated and treated in a biopile to meet CSR Park Land use standards

Excavated areas are refilled with compacted glacial till silt to repair the liner 


Testing water quality in existing monitoring wells on-site

Groundwater sampling is conducted twice a year


LTF expansion

Expansion of the LTF within the current lease boundary to accommodate future soil treatment needs 

Clearing of up to 9 ha, with trees piled and burned on site or salvaged for fuel wood (≥10 cm)
A compacted glacial till silt liner will be added to the cleared area
Vegetation clearing will occur outside of the bird nesting season (May 1 to July 31)
 If clearing must occur within the migratory bird nesting season, then nest surveys will be completed by a qualified biologist prior to clearing 


Closure and decommissioning

Closure and decommissioning are not included in the current project scope; Any future decision to close the facility would be made jointly by Arctic Backhoe and the Yukon Government, in accordance with Permit 24-002 and applicable Yukon Government protocols, including soil sampling and reporting requirements


Quarry Operation

Production of 3 000 to 10 000 m3/year of material (including pit run, crushed basecourse, bedding sand, bedding stone, blast rock, absorption field drain) 
Pit faces in active areas will typically be up to 5 m in depth
On-site crushing

Twice per/year, for periods of up to three weeks 
7:00 a.m. to 11:00 p.m. 

Routine activities such as excavation, screening, hauling, and sorting will take place seasonally as needed

Excavation hours of operations: 7:00 am to 11:00 pm 

Use of a diesel-powered mobile screening plant to sort and stockpile materials periodically based on demand

Operates from a Power Van and situated on a foundation of compacted pit floor
Sand stockpiles up to 1 000 m3 
 Crushed material up to 3 000 m3 

Sorting oversized rocks
Occasional blasting and sorting of rock
Transport of excavated materials to construction sites based on demand
Reclamation

Periodic review of the development plan to identify areas no longer required for extraction or storage

Recontouring of inactive areas to match surrounding terrain, with final slopes no steeper than 3:1
Redistribution of usable overburden from existing stripping stockpiles to promote natural revegetation
Decommissioning of unneeded access roads through construction of berms or water bars, as appropriate

Consultation with Yukon Lands Branch to confirm additional reclamation requirements, if any


Fuel Management

No stationary fuel storage on site 
Excavator refueled using a truck-mounted Tidy Tank (up to 600 L)
All other vehicles fueled at a commercial station in Whitehorse or elsewhere in Yukon
At least one spill kit carried on vehicles used for refueling at any given time

Personnel

Up to six employees, all of which are accommodated locally in the City of Whitehorse
The current lease allows for a caretaker at the site and currently, there is single accommodation on-site for this purpose

Waste Management 

All waste produced from the project would be managed in accordance with the Yukon Wildlife Act, to prevent attracting dangerous and nuisance wildlife, such as bears
No waste stored onsite; any waste that is generated during operations is to be removed from site and disposed of at the end of each workday 
Secure bin to dispose of waste at the caretaker residence 

Heavy Equipment Use
Other

Wildlife and Wildlife Habitat

Lagoons on site are constructed, with sloped berms and a shallow, flat floor, in a manner to allow for egress from the lagoon should wildlife enter

Aquatic Resources

The Proponent/contractor would at all times have on site sufficient spill cleanup equipment and materials in readiness to immediately contain and cleanup all deleterious substances which may be spilled and be familiar with spill protocols, as contained in the Spill Contingency Plan
Equipment would be cleaned of excess soil from buckets and tracks prior to being used in an area outside the LTF
Vehicles and equipment would be maintained in good working condition to minimize risk of spills or leaks
Where there is a potential for soil erosion, exposed soils will be revegetated with native species to enhance soil stability, and armouring or other techniques may be employed to mitigate soil erosion if required
Deleterious substances (including but not limited to fuels, lubricants, hydraulics, and coolants) of any type would not be deposited or allowed to be deposited into any waters, or in any place where the deleterious substances may enter any waters

Wetlands

Activities in wetlands will be avoided and no wetland disturbance will occur

Heritage Resources

If required, heritage resource assessment would be completed prior to clearing for continued LTF development
Should any artifacts or heritage resource items of significance be uncovered during project groundworks, these activities would be stopped immediately and TKC, KDFN, and YG Heritage Resources Branch would be notified. Groundworks would not resume until the items of significance had been recovered
No artifacts or objects would be removed from site by the contractor or other individuals, other than those permitted to do so

Other Users

Arctic Backhoe has a good working relationship with the quartz mineral claim holders and will communicate with the claim holders about any plans that the Proponent has for the Project
projectScope.summary:
The Project is the continued operation and maintenance of the Arctic Backhoe Ltd. land treatment facility and quarry on a lease of 28 ha.  The land treatment facility associated with the Project will continue receiving and remediating hydrocarbon contaminated soils, sludge, snow, and liquids in accordance with the Contaminated Sites Regulation. The quarry involves the extraction, screening, crushing, sorting, and hauling of aggregate materials.
Proposed activities will occur year round for approximately 40 years. The Project is located within the municipal boundaries of Whitehorse on McLean Lake Road, approximately 2.8 km from the Alaska Highway. It is within the Traditional Territories of the Kwanlin Dün First Nation and Ta’an Kwäch’än Council.

projectScope.summary:
The Project is an amendment to an existing class 4 placer mining operation on 93 claims on Black Hills Creek and its tributaries within the Stewart River Watershed, previously assessed by the Designated Office under YESAB Project 2024-0165. This amendment includes the addition of the Unnamed Right Limit Tributary 2 (UNRLT 2) to be added as a water acquisition location. 
The Project is located approximately 94 km southeast of Dawson City and is located within the Territories of the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyӓk Dun and Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in. The Project falls within Land Management Unit 11 (Goldfields) of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan. Access to the Project area is via the existing Hunker–Granville loop from Dawson City. Amended project activities are proposed from April 15 to October 15 annually, for the duration of the water licence.
